content.text: MTTD, MTTA, and MTTR Tracking for GenAI Incidents

Mean Time to Detect (MTTD), Mean Time to Acknowledge (MTTA), and Mean Time to Resolve (MTTR) are the foundational reliability metrics for any production system. For GenAI platforms, these metrics require careful adaptation because AI-specific failure modes — silent quality degradation, probabilistic errors, compound pipeline latency — make accurate measurement harder than in traditional software systems.

Precise definitions matter because ambiguity in what each metric measures leads to teams optimising for the measurement rather than the underlying reliability. Define the measurement boundaries explicitly in your incident tracking system.
MTTD is the average time from when a failure begins affecting the system to when the on-call team is aware that something is wrong. The start time is the
, not the time an alert fires.
For traditional software bugs, failure onset is usually a deploy or configuration change with a known timestamp. For GenAI incidents, failure onset is often unclear:
Model drift begins at an unknown point in time as the data distribution shifts
A hallucination pattern may have been present since the model checkpoint but only surfaces under specific user prompts
A retrieval quality degradation may start when an index is rebuilt with stale data, hours before queries start returning wrong results
When the true failure onset is ambiguous, use the
as the start time: the first metric, log line, or user report that would have indicated something was wrong if someone had been looking. Document the uncertainty in the postmortem.
MTTA is the average time from when an alert fires to when the on-call engineer acknowledges it. This measures the operational responsiveness of the on-call process, not the detection system.

MTTA is measured from alert fire time, not from failure onset. It is a pure measure of human response speed and on-call process health. A long MTTA indicates that alerts are being ignored (alert fatigue), the on-call rotation is not properly configured, or escalation paths are broken.
MTTR has two variants that should be tracked separately:
: time from failure onset to when user impact is stopped or significantly reduced. This is the operational MTTR — what the on-call team controls.
: time from failure onset to when the root cause is fixed and the system is fully restored. This may be days after the incident is 'resolved' for users.
For most SLO calculations and customer communications, use MTTR (mitigation). For reliability improvement tracking, use MTTR (resolution).

attrs.alt: MTTD, MTTA, MTTR timeline for GenAI incidents

content.text: The hardest MTTD problem in AI systems is when quality degrades without any infrastructure metric changing. Error rates stay flat, latency stays normal, and request volumes are unchanged — but the model is producing worse outputs. This can persist for hours or days before a user report, evaluation metric, or A/B test surfaces it.
Mitigation: add model-quality metrics that are evaluated continuously, not just at deployment time:

# Continuous quality sampling: evaluate a fixed probe set every N minutes
# and alert if pass rate drops below threshold

PROBE_QUESTIONS = [
    # Representative queries across the quality dimensions you care about
    {"input": "Summarise this document in 3 bullet points: ...", "eval_fn": "check_bullet_count"},
    {"input": "What is the capital of France?", "eval_fn": "check_factual_accuracy"},
    {"input": "Write a Python function to reverse a list.", "eval_fn": "check_code_executes"},
]

async def continuous_quality_probe():
    results = []
    for probe in PROBE_QUESTIONS:
        response = await model.complete(probe["input"])
        passed = EVAL_FUNCTIONS[probe["eval_fn"]](response)
        results.append(passed)

    pass_rate = sum(results) / len(results)
    metrics.gauge("model.quality.probe_pass_rate", pass_rate)

    if pass_rate < 0.85:
        alert("model_quality_degraded", severity="SEV-2",
              details=f"Probe pass rate {pass_rate:.0%} below 85% threshold")
With continuous quality probes, MTTD for quality failures drops from hours to minutes — the probe fires before any user notices.

A model that fails 2% of requests is easy to miss with standard alerting thresholds set for 5xx error rates. The 2% failure rate may be within the alert's noise band, especially if the baseline error rate is 1%.
For AI platforms, set per-capability error rate alerts with appropriate baselines:

# Alert on elevated error rate relative to rolling baseline
# rather than against a fixed threshold

alerts:
  - name: inference_error_rate_elevated
    query: |
      rate(inference_errors_total[5m])
      / rate(inference_requests_total[5m])
    condition: >
      current_rate > (rolling_7d_p95_rate * 2.0)
    # Fires when error rate is 2x the normal 95th-percentile rate
    # Adapts to seasonal patterns automatically
    severity: SEV-2
    annotations:
      summary: "Inference error rate {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} is 2x baseline"

  - name: inference_error_rate_critical
    condition: >
      current_rate > 0.05   # absolute floor: always alert above 5%
    severity: SEV-1

A GenAI request typically flows through retrieval, re-ranking, prompt construction, inference, and post-processing. When latency degrades, attributing the slowdown to the correct stage is essential for accurate MTTR — the wrong diagnosis leads to the wrong fix.
Instrument each pipeline stage with its own latency histogram and propagate a request ID through the entire pipeline:

import time
from opentelemetry import trace

tracer = trace.get_tracer("ai-pipeline")

async def handle_rag_request(query: str, request_id: str):
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("rag_pipeline") as span:
        span.set_attribute("request_id", request_id)

        with tracer.start_as_current_span("retrieval"):
            t0 = time.monotonic()
            docs = await retriever.search(query)
            metrics.histogram("pipeline.retrieval.duration",
                              time.monotonic() - t0)

        with tracer.start_as_current_span("reranking"):
            t0 = time.monotonic()
            ranked = reranker.rank(query, docs)
            metrics.histogram("pipeline.reranking.duration",
                              time.monotonic() - t0)

        with tracer.start_as_current_span("inference"):
            t0 = time.monotonic()
            response = await llm.complete(build_prompt(query, ranked))
            metrics.histogram("pipeline.inference.duration",
                              time.monotonic() - t0)

        return response

With per-stage metrics, an MTTR that would otherwise be consumed by investigation (which stage is slow?) shrinks because the dashboard immediately shows whether the latency spike is in retrieval or inference.
Reference targets depend heavily on the tier of the AI service and the customer expectation. The following are starting-point targets for a customer-facing GenAI API platform:

MTTD targets assume continuous monitoring (alerts, not manual checks). If your alerting coverage has gaps, your effective MTTD is the gap duration plus the alert-to-acknowledge time — set targets only after knowing your actual coverage.
Accurate MTTx metrics require a structured data collection process. Every incident should record four timestamps:

# Incident metadata schema (enforce via incident creation form)
incident:
  id: INC-20240315-001
  severity: SEV-2
  title: "RAG retrieval latency degraded"

  timestamps:
    failure_onset: "2024-03-15T14:02:00Z"   # when did the failure actually start?
    detected_at: "2024-03-15T14:09:00Z"      # when did the alert fire or first signal appear?
    acknowledged_at: "2024-03-15T14:11:30Z"  # when did on-call engineer ACK?
    mitigated_at: "2024-03-15T15:38:00Z"     # when was user impact stopped?
    resolved_at: "2024-03-15T17:45:00Z"      # when was root cause fixed?

  computed:
    mttd_minutes: 7      # detected_at - failure_onset
    mtta_minutes: 2.5    # acknowledged_at - detected_at
    mttr_mitigation_minutes: 96   # mitigated_at - failure_onset
    mttr_resolution_minutes: 223  # resolved_at - failure_onset
Manual timestamp collection is unreliable under stress. Automate what you can:
: pull from monitoring system's anomaly start time if available; fall back to deploy timestamp or human estimate with uncertainty flag
: pulled automatically from the alert manager when the incident is created from an alert
: captured automatically when the on-call engineer clicks 'Acknowledge' in PagerDuty or equivalent
: set manually when the incident commander declares mitigation; require this before allowing status page to show 'Monitoring'
: set manually when the IC closes the incident; require a short resolution summary
Tracking MTTx is only valuable if the numbers drive decisions. The standard improvement cycle:
Plot rolling 90-day P50 and P90 for each metric by severity. Look for:
: alerts are missing failures or the failure surface is growing faster than monitoring coverage
: alert fatigue, rota gaps, or escalation paths broken
: runbooks are stale, on-call engineers lack permissions or context, or the system is harder to restore
: action items are not closing, root causes are more complex, or teams are under-resourced on reliability work

Segment MTTx by root-cause category to identify where to invest. A team with good overall MTTD but terrible MTTD for model-quality failures knows exactly where to invest: quality evaluation metrics and probes.

# Example: segment MTTD by root-cause category
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame(incidents)
summary = (
    df.groupby("root_cause_category")["mttd_minutes"]
    .agg(["median", "mean", lambda x: x.quantile(0.9)])
    .rename(columns={"<lambda_0>": "p90"})
    .sort_values("median", ascending=False)
)
print(summary)
# root_cause_category    median    mean    p90
# model-quality          182.3    240.1   480.0  <- fix quality monitoring
# capacity               8.2      12.4    24.0
# integration            5.1       7.2    18.0

MTTx metrics and error budget consumption are directly linked. An error budget represents the acceptable amount of unreliability over a window (e.g., 0.1% of requests can fail per 30 days). Every incident consumes error budget proportional to its duration and impact.
The relationship between MTTR and error budget burn rate:

def error_budget_consumed_by_incident(
    affected_request_fraction: float,  # e.g. 0.30 = 30% of requests affected
    mttr_hours: float,
    total_window_hours: float = 720,   # 30 days
    slo_target: float = 0.999          # 99.9% availability
) -> float:
    """
    Returns the fraction of the monthly error budget consumed by one incident.
    """
    allowed_downtime_hours = total_window_hours * (1 - slo_target)  # = 0.72h for 99.9%
    effective_downtime = mttr_hours * affected_request_fraction

    return effective_downtime / allowed_downtime_hours

# Example: 30-min SEV-1 affecting 100% of traffic at 99.9% SLO
consumed = error_budget_consumed_by_incident(
    affected_request_fraction=1.0,
    mttr_hours=0.5
)
print(f"Budget consumed: {consumed:.0%}")  # -> 69%

This calculation makes explicit why MTTR improvement is the highest-leverage reliability investment: halving MTTR from 60 minutes to 30 minutes saves nearly the same error budget as doubling the SLO window.

A single 60-minute SEV-1 blows the entire monthly error budget at 99.9%. This is why MTTR targets for SEV-1 are set at 30 minutes or less: anything longer and one incident consumes the full month's reliability allowance.

MTTA is underrated as a process health metric. It does not measure system reliability — it measures whether the on-call process is working. Patterns in MTTA data reveal operational problems that no amount of alerting investment can fix:
Rota gap (no on-call engineer in that timezone)
Expand rota coverage or set escalation path
Engineers respond fast but alerts fire late
Invest in monitoring coverage, not on-call speed
SEV-3 alerts not paging; treated as tickets
Add explicit escalation path for stale SEV-3

Review MTTA by engineer and by time-of-day monthly. Alert fatigue almost always manifests as high MTTA during off-hours when engineers are less motivated to respond quickly to an alert they have been conditioning themselves to ignore.

MTTx targets should be set against both internal historical trends and industry benchmarks. For AI/ML platform teams, published benchmarks are sparse, but some reference points from reliability engineering practice:
Google SRE teams target MTTR (mitigation) under 30 minutes for Tier-1 services
AWS and Azure publish per-service availability data; infer MTTR from outage duration reports in their status histories
For GenAI specifically: quality degradation incidents (model drift, evaluation gap) typically have MTTD 10-100x longer than infrastructure incidents because they lack hard error signals

The most useful benchmark comparison is not against other companies but against your own team's historical data. Set initial targets at your current P50 (to establish the baseline) and improvement targets at your historical P25 (what your best quarters looked like).
Only counting incidents where an alert fired; ignoring user-reported issues
Count all incidents; MTTD = infinity if detection was a user report
Fast acknowledgement but slow detection; team looks responsive while quality degrades
MTTD is the more impactful metric; focus on alert coverage first
Closing incidents when user impact stops, before root cause is fixed
Track both timestamps separately; MTTR (resolution) cannot be closed without a fix
Reporting single MTTR number; masks that SEV-1 is fine but SEV-3 has no tracking at all
Always segment by severity; report P50 and P90, not just mean

MTTD, MTTA, and MTTR are the three primary reliability process metrics for GenAI incident management. MTTD captures detection speed from actual failure onset, not alert fire time. MTTA measures on-call process responsiveness. MTTR should be tracked in two forms: mitigation (user impact stopped) and resolution (root cause fixed). GenAI-specific challenges — silent quality degradation, probabilistic failure rates, pipeline stage attribution — require instrumentation beyond standard infrastructure monitoring: continuous quality probes, baseline-relative alert thresholds, and per-stage distributed tracing. Targets should be set by severity tier and reviewed monthly as rolling 90-day P50/P90 trends, segmented by root-cause category to drive specific improvement investments.
MTTD starts at failure onset, not alert fire time -- the gap between them is your detection blind spot
Track MTTR (mitigation) and MTTR (resolution) separately -- they measure different things
AI quality failures need continuous probes; error rates alone will not catch silent degradation
Segment MTTx by root-cause category to identify where monitoring or runbook investment is needed
P90 matters more than mean -- optimise the tail, not just the average incident
